steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank sits between "novel" and "Gothic", where "Mexican Gothic" is the title of Moreno-Garcia's novel. The question tests whether any punctuation should appear between the noun "novel" and the title that directly names it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'novel (Mexican': Opens a parenthesis that is never closed within the sentence, creating a punctuation error. ✗
  • Choice B — 'novel Mexican': No punctuation between "novel" and its title — correct, because a title used as a direct identifier of the noun requires no boundary punctuation. ✓
  • Choice C — 'novel: Mexican': A colon after "novel" would require "novel" to introduce what follows as a list or explanation, but "Mexican Gothic (2020) earning a spot..." is a participial phrase modifying the noun phrase, not an independent clause or listed item. ✗
  • Choice D — 'novel, Mexican': A comma here would suggest "Mexican" is a non-restrictive modifier set off from the noun, but the title "Mexican Gothic" is essential to identifying which novel is meant. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
